text: Removal of radiological iodine isotopes (129I) is an ongoing challenge for both nuclear waste storage as well as nuclear energy generation. Silver containing mordenite (MOR) remains one of the gold standard materials for iodine sorption removal. With the emergence of nuclear fuel reprocessing, complex mixed gas streams are expected to be encountered which will demand high selectivity for all species of iodine species to ensure safe and environmentally friendly remediation. One of the major knowledge gaps concerns the sorption behavior of organic iodides, where the underlying molecular mechanism is currently poorly understood. Our study investigates the behavior of methyl iodide toward the silver sites of mordenite using a suite of techniques including: FTIR, TGA-Mass Spec (MS), Micro-X-ray Fluorescence (XRF), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) as well as pair distribution function (PDF) analysis to elucidate the structure and distribution of these species in the Ag-MOR material and to understand the respective capture behavior. Our combined data indicate the iodide is held within the pores of the MOR in the form of silver iodide nanoparticles consistent with the calculated pore diameters of the material. This indicates both a strong physical as well as a high degree of chemical binding of the iodine species to the sorbent. The distribution of the alkyl portion of methyl iodide is observed to be surface associated with the zeolite, catalytically transforming the molecule into SMS (Surface Methoxy Species). Subsequent release of subsequent species is into dimethyl ether and methanol.
